title: A list of pairs
testCase: Did you turn the specs into a <dl> with <dt> and <dd>?
blocks.0.text: You are adding the bike specs. You made them a list — looks fine. But look at what you actually wrote.
blocks.1.text: Every item is TWO things glued with a colon: the name of a property and its value. "Frame" is the question, "aluminium" is the answer. In the code they are one thing — plain text where a colon carries all the meaning. A colon is punctuation. It means nothing to a machine.
blocks.2.text: Try a thought experiment: you want every property name to come out bold. There is no way to grab them. They do not exist.
blocks.4.items.1: <dt> — the term, the name. The question.
blocks.4.items.2: <dd> — the description, the value. The answer.
blocks.7.text: No bullets. The answer is indented under its question. And most importantly: now the two things ARE two things. The colon is gone, because it is no longer needed — the relationship lives in the code, not in the punctuation.
blocks.9.items.0: <ul> — things of one kind, order does not matter. Ingredients, parts, features.
blocks.9.items.1: <ol> — the order IS the meaning. Steps, rankings, instructions.
blocks.9.items.2: <dl> — pairs. Specs, a glossary, questions and answers, metadata.
blocks.10.text: One <dt> can have several <dd> — a word with two meanings. And several <dt> can share one <dd> — two synonyms with one explanation. Do not memorise this; remember it when you need it.
blocks.11.text: When you see a colon inside a list item, stop. The list is probably the wrong one.
blocks.12.text: Your turn. Turn the specs into a description list — without a single colon.
